notes: Run 1. Copilot used curl via terminal instead of fetch_webpage, saving raw HTML rather than converted markdown. File is 496KB raw HTML vs 7KB markdown from preliminary runs — fundamentally different retrieval mechanism. MD5 match confirms identical file between Copilot and verifier. Verifier counts 0 code blocks, table rows, and headers because counting logic targets markdown syntax not HTML tags; web page does contain all three. Copilot counted these from its internal representation of the HTML, not the saved file, explaining the discrepancy. Line count off by 1 (97 vs 98), likely trailing newline. Hypothesis H1-no because full raw HTML was retrieved — fetch_webpage size ceiling did not apply when curl bypassed it. This run demonstrates that tool substitution fundamentally changes what is measured.

Run 2. Copilot used curl via terminal instead of fetch_webpage, saving raw HTML. Copilot went off-script and created two files during the run — raw_output_BL-1_headers.txt containing response headers and raw_output_BL-1.txt containing the body — then removed the headers file before finalizing, so only the body was saved as the artifact. MD5 match confirms identical file. File is 496KB raw HTML vs 7KB markdown from run 1. Verifier counts 0 for code blocks, table rows, and headers because counting logic targets markdown syntax; Copilot correctly identified these as HTML elements and reported 0 markdown code blocks, 28 HTML dt/tr rows, 4 HTML h5-h6 tags. Line count matched (98 vs 98). Token discrepancy: Copilot claimed tiktoken and reported 180,061; verifier measured 177,388 using cl100k_base — difference of 2,673 tokens suggests different encoding or whitespace handling. H1-no because curl bypassed fetch_webpage size ceiling and retrieved full raw HTML. Tool substitution is consistent with run 1 and produces a fundamentally different artifact than fetch_webpage would.
tools_used: curl -> zsh -> pylanceRunCodeSnippet(pylance mcp server) -> wc -> grep -> hexdump

notes: Run 3. First run where fetch_webpage was used instead of curl — produces 7KB markdown-converted output vs 496KB raw HTML from runs 1 and 2, confirming fetch_webpage performs server-side HTML-to-markdown conversion and filters navigation and boilerplate. Copilot explicitly characterized this as delivering approximately 8.4% of the original 85KB page size, framing it as bounded excerpt retrieval not truncation. MD5 match confirms identical file. Code block discrepancy explained: the JSON example on the page is returned by fetch_webpage as inline backtick-escaped formatting rather than triple-backtick fenced blocks, so both Copilot and verifier correctly report 0 fenced code blocks — but for different reasons than the HTML runs. The file viewer showing apparent code structure on lines 66-69 is backtick-inline not fenced-block syntax. Verifier script does not need changes. Token discrepancy: Copilot used 4-char heuristic and estimated 1,790; verifier measured 1,721 with actual cl100k_base tiktoken — expected imprecision from heuristic. Line count off by 1 (184 vs 185), likely trailing newline handling. Table rows and headers match exactly (15 and 26). H1-yes: fetch_webpage ceiling applies here, content was filtered and compressed to 8.4% of source.
tools_used: fetch_webpage -> wc -> md5 -> grep -> tail -> python3 -c

notes: Run 4. GPT model family outputs raw HTML without HTML-to-markdown conversion, contrasting with Claude model behavior in run 3. Full 496.76KB file retrieved with no truncation. MD5 match confirms identical file integrity. Copilot self-reported word count 80668 wildly overestimated versus verified 15370 - GPT appears to count HTML tag attributes and whitespace-separated tokens as words rather than prose words. Line count matches exactly at 98. All structure counts at 0 because verifier script detects markdown syntax (fenced code blocks, markdown tables, markdown headers) not HTML syntax - raw HTML structure is not captured by current verification approach. Token count 177388 via cl100k_base shows 2.87 chars/token ratio on HTML vs typical 3.5-4.0 for prose, expected for markup-heavy content. No truncation indicators detected, file ends cleanly with closing HTML tags. H1-no: no character-based truncation ceiling applied, GPT models retrieve full source without conversion or filtering.
tools_used: run_terminal -> curl -> stat -> md5 -> wc -> python3 -> tail -> xxd -> echo

notes: Run 5. GPT model family outputs raw HTML without conversion, identical to run 4. Full 496.76KB retrieval with no truncation. MD5 match confirms file integrity. Copilot saved separate headers file showing HTTP response metadata including accept-ranges: bytes, which indicates the server supports HTTP range requests for partial content retrieval. This suggests GPT models could theoretically request byte ranges, though no evidence of chunked retrieval observed - full document downloaded in single request. Copilot self-reported counts match verifier this time: 508686 chars, 98 lines, 15370 words, 177388 tokens at 2.87 chars/token. Structure count divergence remains: Copilot detected HTML structures (1 code block, 31 table rows, 4 headers) while verifier finds 0 for all because it looks for Markdown syntax not HTML tags. Server metadata shows CloudFront CDN delivery, Netlify Edge hosting, cache-status Miss from cloudfront, etag present. Content delivered as raw bytes with UTF-8 charset. No truncation indicators, file ends cleanly with closing HTML tags. H1-no: no character-based truncation ceiling, GPT retrieves full source without filtering.
tools_used: run_terminal -> curl -> mcp_pylanceRunCodeSnippet -> wc -> md5 -> python3
